sentences.text: [SIGHS] Yes.
And so then again, you know, after-- then people--
Yes.
There was-- there was-- there were some other people, yes.
I don't remember how many.
And I remember where I saw some people are-- took off their jackets.
And they threw it up on the air so they can see that we are not soldiers, you know?
And apparently, they-- they did notice that-- and they stopped.
They stopped the shooting.
And they went away.
And everybody, you know, again, they took the dead and the wounded, threw them in in a boxcar.
And-- and the others went into their car.
And so again, we're still going, still traveling-- we don't know where.
And a couple days later, again, the train stops.
The train stops.
They all-- you know, they open up the-- the box, you know-- open up the boxcars.
And, you know, we look around.
And we even dared to go down.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we see-- we see-- we used to have some young soldiers and older soldiers.
And we noticed that the young ones disappeared.
And we were left with the older ones.
Yeah, German soldiers.
And they-- they were not so strict.
They didn't yell at us-- get back to the car, get back to the car.
You know?
Yes.
Yes, as a matter of fact, I did.
And all of a sudden, I see a truck.
So I came to a tree.
And I was hiding behind the tree.
I saw it was a military truck.
But you know, I thought it was the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] The truck stops.
I saw-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --that they were different.
You know, they weren't-- they weren't Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So we told them about, you know, that there's a train with a lot of people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So he said, go down there a little further, and you will see a lot of American soldiers.
And they will give you food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we went down there.
And then later on, a lot of other people from there went there.
But what did they-- what they had to give us is canned food-- beef, you know.
And our stomach was not used to food or greasy food.
And a lot of people died.
A lot of people died right after the liberation, because they couldn't-- they couldn't-- they couldn't take-- you know, they ate too much or whatever, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Well, I-- I ate, but-- but you know, I didn't get-- I didn't get sick.
I didn't get sick.
And we go, and we don't-- we still don't know what to do.
We are running around-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Well, nobody special.
But we-- I got together with some other people, and we walked together.
And what they did is they rounded us up, little by little.
And they took us to this camp.
They make-- they made out of the Hitler Youth Camp, they made a displaced-persons camp at a place called Feldafing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
That was-- that was about the beginning of May.
And so they gave us food.
You know, they made sure that, you know, again, they made sure that we don't overeat, and we eat the right food, and drink the right amount of water, you know?
And we were being examined, you know?
And we are being treated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yes.
Yes.
Yeah, you know, most of us, you know, we were sick.
You know, we--
Undernourished, yeah, undernourished.
Oh, who knows?
But I didn't weigh very much.
Yeah.
I have no idea in those days, no.
And probably, I was probably there-- I don't remember-- maybe two months, roughly speaking.
Then they asked people if they want to go back to their-- to their home.
And they made up transports to go home.
And I said-- decided, yes, I-- I want to go home.
And we went to-- we passed through Czechoslovakia, Prague.
And they gave us food.
But we didn't stay there.
We kept on going.
But we did stop in Slovakia, in Bratislava.
And they gave us a-- a hotel.
You know, we stayed in a hotel.
And we-- we were there a few days.
And that's where I met my brother.
And it wasn't me, actually--
Well, there was-- there was a couple of girls from my hometown.
And going in from the hotel into downtown, they saw him.
And they say-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH].
His name is [NON-ENGLISH].
He says, hey!
Your brother is with us.
He-- he-- come-- come with me to the hotel.
And they brought him over.
And-- and then on the way, when I was going back to the hotel-- people were going back and forth, back and forth-- so somebody else from that group went to downtown, and they saw me.
He says, you go into our room, and we have a surprise for you.
I say, well, what is the surprise?
Oh, I'm not gonna tell you.
And I forced-- I forced her to tell me.
And she said, your brother is waiting for you.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
So we met.
And we kissed, and we hugged.
And we stayed together and then went back home.
So the first thing you have to do is you have to register with them for an ID.
Register with whom?
With-- with the-- with the-- with-- with the police.
Yeah.
And they gave you an ID.
So they say, well, we gonna give you a week to settle yourself down.
Find yourself a place where you're gonna live.
And then you come back over here and report for work.
We're gonna to give you a job.
Well, that-- that was-- that was-- that was at that time, it was under Russia-- Russian occupation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, yes, sir.
Yes, sir.
As soon as we walk out of there, I said to my brother, I say, we're not gonna stay here very long.
We gonna be leaving.
So even so, my brother is two years older, but I took charge.
Because--
--because I-- I felt that I-- I am more capable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
What?
Well-- how shall I say this?
He-- I saw that he was more-- he-- he got more affected by the war.
And his thinking was not as clear as mine.
So I knew that I had to take charge.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
How old I was at that time?
I was 17 going on 18.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So he said, well, where are we gonna go?
I says, first, we're gonna go to Budapest.
And then we'll go to-- we'll go to Palestine.
That was even before it-- Israel became a state, you know.
Yes.
So it was Palestine.
We had-- we couldn't think of going to America, because we didn't have anybody here.
And-- and you to go to America, somebody have to send you a visa.
So the only other place was left was Palestine.
They didn't come back.
They didn't come back.
I did meet somebody when I got home to Munkatch.
I met somebody who was in the same camp with my father.
And what he told me is what happened that he got bitten by an SS dog at-- on the work-- you know, the place of work.
And he was sent-- they couldn't take care of him there.
It was a small camp.
So they send him to Dachau.
And that was the last time they saw him.
So he-- he died over there.
The mother, I don't know what exactly whether-- how soon after she died.
Nobody-- nobody ever told me that she wound up in the camp first or whatever.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, the-- the train station was bombed a little bit.
And also the bridge was bombed.
We had a nice bridge, and that was bombed.
And I think that's about the only thing that was bombed.
My home, somebody else was living there already.
I went to look at it, yeah.
But I didn't want to stay there, anyway.
I didn't want to stay there.
I don't know.
I don't know.
No.
No.
I just passed-- just passed by, you know?
There's a-- the house.
Well, I felt that it's-- it's-- it's no longer my home.
And I knew that I'm not gonna make this my home, this city.
I want out.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So we did go to Budapest.
Were there any Jews left in Munkatch?
Yes.
Yes, there were some Jews left.
I mean, you know, they went home.
And they remained there, what I'm trying to say.
They didn't make--
They-- they went back, and they made a--
--they went back, and they made it their home, yes.
They started to build a life again.
Yeah.
And--
Pardon me?
I have no idea.
But I-- I-- I do know-- I do know one fella, that he is-- he is here now back, 21-- he-- he's here 21 years.
So he stayed there about approximately about 25 years or so-- 25, 26 years after the war.
And he said there's a lot of people.
They-- they came back, and they got married.
And they had children.
And then they lived there.
But I didn't-- I didn't want to stay.
I said, I'm not gonna stay here.
Nothing to stay there for.
As a matter of fact-- so in Budapest, when we got to Budapest, we joined-- we joined a kibbutz.
And they made up transports to go to Palestine.
And everything went illegally, you know.
And some of the ships-- there were some ships that were sunk.
Others were captured and taken to Cyprus.
Well, what happened to me is, first of all, the-- the people who made up the transports, they separated me and my brother.
They say-- they say, because, you see, I-- I understand now them-- the reason for it is because if something should happen, one should remain alive.
So they said to me-- he says, you go first.
That our-- that our family shouldn't disappear.
Yeah, right.
This is what I hear.
This is what I understood.
Actually, nobody told me this.
But I-- I figured that out.
Yes.
Yes.
So they sent me first.
And I went as far as Austria.
And from Austria, we were supposed to go to-- to Italy.
And from Italy, we were supposed to go to Palestine.
So I waited and waited.
It was a long procedure.
And one day, I see a bulletin-- 'Children under 18 should register.' And they'll take 'em to America.
But belonging to a kibbutz and going to meetings, you become indoctrinated to go to Palestine, to build a homeland.
So when I saw the-- that bulletin, I didn't rush right away to register.
And in fact, the thought occurred to me to tear that down, that other children my age and younger shouldn't see it-- shouldn't be tempted to go to America.
But instead, what I-- I didn't tear it down.
That evening, when I went to sleep, I was doing some thinking.
I says, who knows how much longer am I gonna have to be here before I can go?
And I wanted to get out of Europe in the worst way.
That's when I made up my mind.
I'm gonna register.
But with this in mind, wherever is gonna come up first, that's where I will go, as long as I get out of here.
So it came up for me to go to America first.
So this is-- so I-- I-- I wind up over here.
And it took me-- from the time we separated in Budapest, with my brother-- it took me five years to get in touch with him again.
And so I wind up-- I lived in Pittsburgh.
And one day, I see-- I was reading a magazine-- the Jewish-- called the It's a Jewish magazine, you know?
There was an ad, children, I mean, people who are still looking for-- for a family should write to New York to this and this address.
And I wrote.
And I gave all the information about my brother.
And at first, I used to get letters from people-- not my brother.
Everybody wanted to have a brother in America.
So--
Well, I asked-- I wrote them back, and I asked questions-- never heard from them again.
Then I received a letter from--
Well, I asked parents' name and where did they live-- never received an answer.
Then I received a letter from my brother.
I knew right away it was him.
I just had a feeling.
Yes.
And you know, of course, I wrote him back and I asked questions.
And I got all the answers I needed.
And I-- I worked in a wholesale men's haberdashery store in Pittsburgh.
And there were three partners.
But I worked on the inside.
I was the inside man with-- with one of the partners.
And they liked me so much that they were willing to send an affidavit to my brother, to bring him over-- send him as a-- as a relative, as a cousin.
You know what I mean?
Why not as a brother?
My-- my boss claimed to be a relative
--of him.
Yes.
Yeah.
Yes.
So-- but my brother, at that time, was just released from the army.
He was in Israel, yeah.
And he was in the reserves.
That was approximately 1950.
Yes.
Yes.
So-- and Israel needed the young people, especially-- especially somebody who already served in the army in the-- in the reserves.
So they made 'em very, very difficult to get out.
In the meantime, he got married.
And he married a girl from Iraq.
And she had a lot of family over there.
So what are you gonna do?
If he gonna to bring 'em both over, then she is going to be separated from her family.
So we all decided that it's best that you stay where you are, and I stay where I am.
And I'll come and visit you.
So I was there already three times.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And--
Ah, so-- so-- then-- so he remained over there.
And you know, I just send them-- I send them packages, send him what he needs and help them out.
And I--
Well, what happened to me is I decided, as much as I liked the people I worked for, and they liked me, and I had a good job-- I became an inside salesman-- and filling the orders.
It was a wholesale men's haberdashery store over there.
And I met up with some other friends.
And we talked it over that we'll go to California-- go to Los Angeles.
So we bought a car together, and we drove across country.
Well, one of them is Eddie, Eddie [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah.
And we're still very good friends.
Yeah.
And we find-- you know, worked on jobs, different jobs, and worked as a-- the first thing, we got a job as a-- door-to-door, selling dishes and silverware.
But I-- my conscience was bothering me that they charged too much money and interest on the balance.
So I just quit that job.
So I worked for a-- other wholesale stores, you know, a haberdashery, and clothing, and costume jewelry.
Then I find a job.
Then I see an ad in the paper where Douglas Aircraft was hiring.
So I made up an -- filled out an application, and I-- I got hired.
As a--
As a stock clerk.
So I worked as a stock clerk and then got into different classifications-- expediter, and this and that, you know.
And then McDonnell Douglas-- then Douglas Aircraft got into financial trouble, so they merged with McDonnell from St.
Louis.
So that's when it became McDonnell Douglas.
And I stayed there until I retired.
Oh, yes.
Yes, of course, I started dating.
I always used to love to dance.
So I do-- I used to go ballroom dancing.
And that's where I met my ex-wife.
I got married in December of 1957.
Yes-- one daughter from that marriage.
And my daughter is now married, happily married.
She's got a good husband.
He is from Canada.
And they have a 9-year-old boy.
His name is Jason Helen.
My daughter's name is Karen.
Yes.
They live in the Valley, yes.
That brings us up to-- pretty much to date, yes.
What kept me going?
You mean--
Well, what kept me going is that you-- one thing you had to do is you had to have hope that some day this is gonna come to an end.
Because once you have nothing to hope for, then you have nothing to live for.
And you'll-- you just fall apart.
You're a goner.
I kept that hope.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah. 'Cause I-- I've seen-- I've seen people-- strong, big people-- and once they gave up hope, they-- they-- they're gone.
They fell apart.
